---
title: "Native Time and Clocks"
subtitle: "Preserve each feature's original timing until a later analysis chooses a common grid."
sidebar: concepts
---

## Start with a concrete timing problem

Suppose an audio recording begins 12.5 seconds after an experiment begins.
Two seconds into the audio is therefore 14.5 seconds into the experiment.
Both numbers are correct, but they answer different questions because they use
different starting points.

| Event | On the audio clock | On the experiment clock |
|---|---:|---:|
| Recording begins | 0.0 s | 12.5 s |
| A word begins two seconds later | 2.0 s | 14.5 s |

A bare value such as “2.0 seconds” is incomplete. To interpret it, you also
need to know which clock supplied zero.

“Native time” means the time coordinates and row spacing produced by the
feature-computing method, before any later interpolation or resampling.

## A clock is a named time origin

`ClockRef` gives a clock a stable name such as `audio`, `stimulus`,
`experiment`, or `scan:run-01`. It is not a stopwatch object and it does not
read the computer's wall clock. It is a label that answers: “Seconds measured
from what origin?”

Every temporal feature object names its clock through
`timebase.reference`. This prevents code from treating audio time, experiment
time, and scan-run time as interchangeable merely because all three are stored
as seconds.

## A clock map translates coordinates

`ClockMap` records how to translate a time on one clock into a time on another.
The rule is:

\[
\text{target time} = (\text{scale} \times \text{source time}) + \text{offset}
\]

Read the rule from left to right:

1. Start with a coordinate on the source clock.
2. Multiply it by `scale`. The usual value is 1, meaning both clocks advance
   at the same rate.
3. Add `offset_s`, the difference between the two zero points in seconds.
4. The result is a coordinate on the target clock.

For the recording above, the scale is 1 and the offset is 12.5 seconds:

```{python}
from natural_features import ClockMap

audio_to_experiment = ClockMap(
    "audio",
    "experiment",
    scale=1.0,
    offset_s=12.5,
)

audio_to_experiment.apply(2.0)
```

The result is 14.5. A scale other than 1 is useful when two recording systems
advance at slightly different rates. For example, a scale of 1.0002 says that
one second on the source clock spans 1.0002 seconds on the target clock. Scale
must always be positive.

## A temporal context keeps several translations consistent

`TemporalContext` is a collection of clock maps that belong together. If it
knows how to translate stimulus time to experiment time and experiment time to
scan time, it can combine those two steps when asked for stimulus-to-scan
coordinates.

```{python}
from natural_features import TemporalContext

context = TemporalContext(
    (
        ClockMap("audio", "experiment", offset_s=12.5),
        ClockMap("experiment", "scan:run-01", offset_s=-10.0),
    )
)

context.convert(2.0, source="audio", target="scan:run-01")
```

The result is 4.5 seconds on the scan-run clock: 2.0 + 12.5 - 10.0. If two
different paths through the stored maps imply different answers, construction
fails with an error. The library will not choose one contradictory answer.

## Support says how much time a row represents

A coordinate alone still does not say whether a row describes an instant or a
span of time. `SupportSpec` records that meaning.

| Support kind | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `point` | The row belongs to one instant. | A video frame timestamped at 3.0 s |
| `window` | Every row summarizes a fixed-width span. | A 30 ms acoustic window centered at 3.0 s |
| `interval` | Each row has its own onset and offset. | A word lasting from 3.0 to 3.4 s |

For a fixed window, the anchor says whether the stored time marks the window's
beginning, center, or end. For an interval, the row stores both bounds
directly. Clock conversion transforms these bounds along with the main time
coordinate.

## Conversion is not resampling

Changing clocks changes coordinate labels; it does not create, delete, or
interpolate feature rows. If a feature has 100 values before conversion, it
has the same 100 values afterward. Only its time coordinates, temporal bounds,
and clock name change.

This distinction matters when several features have different native rates.
An audio feature measured every 10 milliseconds and a language feature
measured once per word should stay at those rates. `FeatureBundle` holds both
objects together with their shared clock maps without forcing them into one
wide table.

`FeatureBundle.temporal_payload()` packages one native feature for a downstream
model. The payload contains its values or events, original row structure,
clock, support, metadata, versioned object description (the schema), and known
clock mappings. A target clock may be requested, but a target sampling grid is
not chosen.

## Decisions the library will not make for you

Clock conversion will not align unrelated clocks without a map. It will not
upsample every feature to the fastest observed rate. It will not make a wide
join across clocks unless the caller supplies a target clock and a join policy.
These refusals prevent numeric timestamps that mean different things from
being combined silently.

`natural_features` also does not choose a repetition time (TR), interpolate features
onto scan samples, convolve an HRF, or build a design matrix. Those are
modeling decisions and belong in the downstream analysis that knows the
scientific question.

## See also

[Keep heterogeneous features on native grids](../cookbook/native-grid-handoff.qmd) · [Native temporal contract](../spec/native-temporal-contract.qmd) · [Feature objects](feature-objects.qmd)
